serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of fried dried squid with fish sauce is approximately 150 g, typically served as an appetizer or side dish alongside steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Squid, mixed species, cooked, fried (FDC ID 175168) and Fish sauce (FDC ID 173267), combined estimation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for fried dried squid with fish sauce glaze. Values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for fried squid (FDC ID 175168) as the primary base with fish sauce (FDC ID 173267) contributing sodium and minor nutrients. The input record's per_100g values (energy 253 kcal, protein 23.3 g, fat 13.3 g, carbohydrate 10 g, sodium 1200 mg) are broadly consistent with this USDA-anchored estimation for a deep-fried dried squid preparation with a high-sodium fish sauce glaze and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium of 1200 mg/100g is plausible given the combination of dried squid (naturally high sodium) and fish sauce.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate using mixed seafood dish with sauce (GI ~55)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried dried squid with fish sauce. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy with mixed seafood dishes containing moderate sugar-based sauces from the University of Sydney GI Database. Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), a GI of 55 falls in the low category. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 15.0) / 100 = 8.3, where carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 15.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 1200 mg per 100 g, driven by dried squid and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this dish; a single 150 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, far exceeding the recommended 2000 mg daily limit in one serving alone.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from sugar-based fish sauce glaze combined with deep-fried preparation and high cholesterol
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid this dish due to the concentrated simple sugars in the fish sauce glaze (6.7 g sugar/100g), the insulin-spiking effect of deep-frying, and the high cholesterol content (200 mg/100g) that worsens metabolic risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1200 mg/100g), elevated cholesterol (200 mg/100g), and deep-fried fat profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish; the combination of very high sodium, elevated dietary cholesterol, saturated fat from deep-frying, and trace trans fat creates a compounding cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 1200 mg/100g exceeds RA threshold; deep-frying generates AGEs; chilli is a nightshade ingredient
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish due to extremely high sodium worsening tissue swelling, AGE formation from deep-frying promoting joint inflammation, and chilli as a nightshade that may trigger flares in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Dried Squid with Fish Sauce (Vietnamese: Khô Mực Chiên Mắm) is a savoury Vietnamese dish in which dried squid is deep-fried until crispy and then tossed in a sticky, caramelised fish sauce glaze typically made from fish sauce, sugar, garlic, and chilli. The result is a dish with a chewy-yet-crispy texture, intensely umami and slightly sweet flavour, and a glossy coating. It is commonly served as an appetizer, drinking snack, or side dish alongside steamed rice in Vietnamese cuisine. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (23.3 g/100g) from the squid, moderate in fat (13.3 g/100g) from deep-frying, and contains 10 g carbohydrate/100g primarily from the sugar glaze. The most clinically significant feature is the extremely high sodium content of 1200 mg per 100 g, contributed by both the dried squid base and the fish sauce, making this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The dish also carries elevated cholesterol (200 mg/100g) and moderate sugar (6.7 g/100g), raising concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. A standard plate serving is approximately 150 g.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for fried dried squid with fish sauce exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for fried squid and fish sauce, and are broadly consistent with the input record's values. Medium confidence is assigned due to the composite estimation approach and the absence of a direct approved-source match.
